name: Scientology Disconnection
description: Scientology Disconnection is the practice within the Church of Scientology where members are encouraged or pressured to cut off contact with individuals deemed "Suppressive Persons" (SPs) or "Potential Trouble Sources" (PTSs). SPs are individuals believed to be actively working against Scientology, while PTSs are those allegedly connected to SPs and therefore susceptible to their negative influence. The policy, officially described as a voluntary practice, aims to protect Scientologists from perceived harmful influences and maintain the integrity of the organization. Critics, however, argue that Disconnection is a coercive tactic used to control members, isolate them from outside perspectives, and silence dissent. They contend that it tears families apart and causes significant emotional distress. The practice originated with L. Ron Hubbard, the founder of Scientology, who outlined the concept in his writings and policies. Proponents within Scientology maintain that Disconnection is a necessary tool for spiritual advancement and protection from harmful influences, citing examples where individuals have reportedly improved their lives after disconnecting. Mainstream scholars and former members often portray Disconnection as a form of social control and manipulation, highlighting the emotional and psychological damage it can inflict. Legal challenges and public scrutiny have brought increased attention to the practice, further fueling the debate over its ethics and impact.
subtitle: Severing ties for 'spiritual advancement'.

claims.claim: Disconnection protects Scientologists from harmful influences.
claims.evidence: Testimonials from Scientologists who claim to have improved their lives after disconnecting from SPs or PTSs.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that these testimonials are often coerced or manufactured and that disconnection creates more harm than good.

claims.evidence: Church of Scientology publications state that disconnection is a personal choice.
claims.criticism: Former members claim that the intense social pressure within Scientology makes it difficult to refuse disconnection requests without facing severe consequences.

claims.claim: SPs and PTSs are a genuine threat to Scientology and its members.
claims.evidence: Church documents outline the characteristics and behaviors of SPs and PTSs, portraying them as actively working to undermine Scientology.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that the SP and PTS labels are used to silence dissent and control members by labeling anyone who questions Scientology as an enemy.

keyFigures.name: L. Ron Hubbard
keyFigures.role: Founder of Scientology and originator of the Disconnection policy

keyFigures.name: Mike Rinder
keyFigures.role: Former senior executive in the Church of Scientology and vocal critic of Disconnection

keyFigures.name: Lawrence Wright
keyFigures.role: Journalist who has written extensively about Scientology and Disconnection

mythologyConnections: Demonization of dissenters (similar to narratives of heretics or rebels in religious mythology)
Purification rituals (removing perceived impurities to maintain spiritual purity)

timeline.event: L. Ron Hubbard begins developing the concepts of Suppressive Persons and Potential Trouble Sources.
The policy of Disconnection becomes formalized within Scientology.
Disconnection remains a controversial practice, subject to ongoing legal challenges and public scrutiny.

sources.author: Mike Rinder
sources.work: A Billion Years: My Escape From a Life in the Highest Ranks of Scientology
sources.title: Mike Rinder

sources.author: Lawrence Wright
sources.work: Going Clear: Scientology, Hollywood, and the Prison of Belief
